coredata.dc:title: Pentosan-derived water-soluble carbon nano dots with substantial fluorescence: Properties and application as a photosensitizer
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Qiong
Li, Wei
Wu, Yanjiao
Huang, Zhanhua
Liu, Shouxin
coredata.dc:description: The hydrothermal carbonization of monosaccharides and polysaccharides is widely used in the production of carbonaceous material with a desired structure. However, the liquid products are regarded as waste and discarded. Here, we report a facile approach for the synthesis of water-soluble carbon nano dots (CNDs) with substantial fluorescence from the liquid by-products of the hydrothermal carbonization of pentosan, thus the by-products of pulp refining. The synthesized CNDs are monodispersed spheres with abundant oxygen-containing groups and they have an average size of 30nm. Quantum yield measurements revealed CNDs with substantial green photoluminescence (PL) without passivation. Additionally, excitation was independent, pH-sensitive and stable. The use of CNDs as a photosensitizer in the CNDs/TiO2 system for methylene blue (MB) degradation under visible light irradiation is attractive. The spectral response range of the CNDs/TiO2 system can be widened from the UV region to a part of the visible light region (400–550nm).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nano dots
Hydrothermal
Pentosan
Photoluminescence
Photosensitizer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Hydrothermal carbonization of pentosan for CNDs
Texture and surface properties of CNDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image and (b) diameter distribution of the CNDs.
(a) XPS survey spectrum, (b) high-resolution C1s peaks and the fitting curves, (c) the FTIR spectrum and (d) the XRD pattern of the as-synthesized CNDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of CNDs, the inset is the photograph of CNDs under ambient light, (b) PL emission and excitation spectra, (c) emission spectra of the CNDs at different excitation wavelengths, (d) photograph of CNDs illuminated by a UV lamp with 365nm light.
Fluorescence microscopy images of CNDs upon excitation at (a) 360nm, (b) 460nm, and (c) 550nm.
PL spectra of CNDs excited at 365nm in different pH solutions.

(a) SEM and corresponding high resolution TEM images of CNDs/TiO2 composite; (b) MB degradation percentage vs time curves; (c) catalytic mechanism for CNDs/TiO2 under visible light irradiation and (d) recycling tests results.
Parameters for the CNDs stability test.

head.ce:title: Pentosan-derived water-soluble carbon nano dots with substantial fluorescence: Properties and application as a photosensitizer

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin 150040, Heilongjiang, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Northeast Forestry University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Water-soluble carbon nano dots (CNDs) with high fluorescence are synthesized from liquid by-products of hydrothermal carbonization of pentosan, the by-products of pulp refining. CNDs can be effectively used as photo sensitizer in the CNDs/TiO2 system for methyl blue (MB) degradation under visible light irradiation, spectral response range of CNDs/TiO2 system can be widen from only UV region to part visible-light region (400–550nm).

The hydrothermal carbonization of monosaccharides and polysaccharides is widely used in the production of carbonaceous material with a desired structure. However, the liquid products are regarded as waste and discarded. Here, we report a facile approach for the synthesis of water-soluble carbon nano dots (CNDs) with substantial fluorescence from the liquid by-products of the hydrothermal carbonization of pentosan, thus the by-products of pulp refining. The synthesized CNDs are monodispersed spheres with abundant oxygen-containing groups and they have an average size of 30nm. Quantum yield measurements revealed CNDs with substantial green photoluminescence (PL) without passivation. Additionally, excitation was independent, pH-sensitive and stable. The use of CNDs as a photosensitizer in the CNDs/TiO2 system for methylene blue (MB) degradation under visible light irradiation is attractive. The spectral response range of the CNDs/TiO2 system can be widened from the UV region to a part of the visible light region (400–550nm).

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent CNDs are widely used in metal probes [1], imaging [2], optical sensors [3], catalysis [4] and in energy fields [5] since their discovery in 2004 [6]. The photoluminescence of CNDs comes from a quantum effect along with different emissive traps on their surface. Compared with the traditional fluorescent nanoparticles in semiconductor quantum dots [7] and metal nanoparticles [8], CNDs overcome disadvantages such as toxicity, high-cost, and undesirable oxidation that limits their widely preparation. They have excellent photostability as well as optical properties, which enables more practical applications [4,9,10]. Most of recently reported CNDs have low quantum yields (<10%) and low water-solubility, which are the challenges in synthesis [2,11].

Oxygen-containing groups have been shown to contribute to substantial photoluminescence and the water-solubility of CNDs [12,13]. The hydrothermal treatment of biomass has been reported to be effective for the production of oxygen-containing groups. Sevilla [14] prepared carbon spheres with a large amount of carbonyl groups and condensed benzene rings with oxygen in the core and reactive/hydrophilic oxygen functionalities on the shell by the hydrothermal carbonization of cellulose. The reaction mechanism consists of a reaction pathway that involves dehydration, aromatization and carbonization. Guo [15] prepared highly fluorescent CNDs using sodium citrate as the raw material via hydrothermal treatment. They are 1.59nm in size with carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on their surface, and they can potentially be used as probes to detect trace amounts of mercury ions. For hydrothermal treatment, much attention has been given to the solid products while the pale yellow liquid has always been ignored. It has been shown that the liquid contains particles from a few nanometres to hundreds of nanometres in size and they are expected to exhibit similar as well as different spectral properties compared with the solid products because of the small size effect.

We previously synthesized carboxyl-rich carbon microspheres (CSp) through the hydrothermal carbonization of pentosan, which is a by-product of pulp refining. CSp has high adsorption capacity toward Pb(II) and Cd(II). In the work reported here, we obtained water-soluble CNDs from the liquid by-products of hydrothermal carbonization. They exhibit excellent photoluminescence with a high quantum yield (24%) and they have abundant oxygen-containing groups on their surface. The visible-light catalytic activity of the obtained CNDs/TiO2 system toward methylene blue (MB) degradation was also investigated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrothermal carbonization of pentosan for CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: A bleach hard Kraft pulp (BHKP) refinery was used as the source of pentosan. Practical production in a factory environment to separate pentosan from BHKP (brightness of 88% ISO, International Standardization Organization, Kindly provided by a paper-making factory in Heilongjiang, China) was simulated in this work. BHKP (10g) was treated with NaOH (250ml, 2moll−1) under a nitrogen atmosphere for 20h at room temperature. The solution was isolated by centrifugation and the pH was adjusted to 2–3 with 2moll−1 HCl. The solution was then precipitated in ethanol and washed three times with ethanol. Finally, the hemicellulose samples were isolated by centrifugation, washed with distilled water until the pH was neutral and then dried before further use.

In a typical experiment, pentosan (1.5g) was dissolved in distilled water (40ml) by ultrasonic agitation over 15min. The solution was added to a stainless-steel autoclave with a volume of 55ml. The autoclave was heated in a furnace at 210°C for 10h. The autoclave was allowed to cool to room temperature. The resulting brown solution was collected by centrifugation at 12,000rpm for 20min to remove black solids twice. The obtained light yellow solution containing the CNDs was subjected to dialysis (1000Da, molecular weight cut off) for about 48h before further characterization.

CNDs/TiO2 composites were synthesized by the sol–gel method [16]. Tetrabutyl titanate (0.5ml) was dissolved in ethanol (20ml) to form aqueous solution A, and then water (9ml) and ammonia (5ml, 28%) were mixed to form aqueous solution B, B was added to A in a drop wise manner with continual stirring. The mixture was left to age for 12h, and dried at 80°C for 6h after centrifugation. TiO2 was prepared by calcining at 500°C for 1h. The above-mentioned TiO2 (0.1g) was added to the CNDs solution (5ml) with stirring over 10min, followed by drying for further use.

A photocatalytic activity test was carried out in a cylindrical quartz photoreactor (275ml) using MB as the model compound and a 380W Xe lamp (510nm, Shanghai Hualun Lamp Factory, China) positioned inside the reactor as the light source. Photocatalyst powder (0.1g) was added to a 100ml aqueous solution (100mgl−1). Before the photocatalytic degradation, the suspension was magnetically stirred in the dark for 30min to establish a MB adsorption/desorption equilibrium. Samples of 5ml were collected from the suspension and were centrifuged. The concentration after illumination was determined spectrophotometrically using a TU-1900 UV–Vis spectrometer at 665nm.

Recycling tests were used to investigate the stability of obtained CNDs/TiO2 composites. After degradation tests, CNDs/TiO2 was collected by centrifugal separation at 8000rpm for 15min, then the solid product was added into 50ml distilled water and magnetic stirred at 30°C for 40min. After desorption, the composite was vacuum dried at 80°C for 5h, then was reused as photocatalyst for another runs of catalytic activity test.

The quantum yields were calculated using 5μgml−1 Rhodamine B in ethanol as a standard (quantum yield 90%). The absorbance of Rhodamine B at 543nm was less than 0.05. The calculation was performed according to the following equation: Φu= Φs(Yu/Ys)(As/Au) where u and s represent the CNDs and Rhodamine B, Φ is the quantum yield, and Y is the integrated fluorescence area. A is the absorption at fluorescence excitation.

Transmission electron microscopy images were obtained on a JEOL 2011 (FEI, Holland) operated at 200kV. X-ray photoelectron spectra were recorded on a Physical Electronics PHI5700 spectrometer (Chanhassen, MN, USA) using Al-Kα radiation (hν =1486.6eV). Binding energies were referenced to the C1s line at 284.6eV. Quantitative analyses of C and O were based on the peak intensities of the C1s and O1s signals, respectively. Diffuse reflectance infrared (IR) spectra of the CNDs were recorded on a PerkinElmer TV1900 Fourier-transform IR (FTIR) spectrometer (Waltham, MA, USA). Powder X-ray diffraction patterns were obtained from a Bruker D4 (Bruker, Japan) power X-ray diffractometer using Cukɑ radiation (40kV, 40mA). UV–vis absorption spectra were carried out using a TU-1900 UV–vis spectrometer. Fluorescence spectroscopy was performed on a LS-55 fluorescence spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA) equipped with a 120W xenon lamp as the excitation source. Fluorescence microscopy images were obtained using an Axioimager A1 microscope (Carl Zeiss, Germany). The excitation wavelengths were 365, 455, 545nm and the light source for fluorescence microscopy observations was a mercury lamp with a fluorescent filter. All the measurements were performed at room temperature. The morphologies and sizes of the composites were observed using environmental scanning electron microscopy (SEM; Quanta 200, FEI, Hillsboro, OR, USA).pH tests were performed using the same CNDs solution concentrations in a pH range from 4 to 10, and these were adjusted using different pH buffer solutions.

Photostability tests were carried out at room temperature, and light at 365nm from an ultraviolet lamp (12W, Beijing, China) was used to irradiate the CNDs, and the fluorescence intensity of the CNDs was measured at different times. The CNDs were treated in a strongly ionic solution (NaCl) with concentrations up to 500μmol/l, and the fluorescence intensity of the CNDs was measured at different concentrations.

The reaction mechanism for the formation of solid products in hydrothermal carbonization has been extensively investigated, and as the CNDs are present in the liquid products they may have the same reaction pathway with similar properties. Since they are small it is probable that some of their characteristics are different to those of the solid products. The proposed synthetic strategy and mechanism is shown in Fig. 1 . The process may involve four stages including dehydration, polymerization, aromatization and carbonization (passivation), as indicated by the many reports on the hydrothermal carbonization of biomass [17,18]. The basic units form macromolecules through intermolecular dehydration and polymerization when the temperature and concentration are increased to a critical supersaturation point. A short single nucleation burst accompanied by aromatization is apparent. Upon nucleation, growth occurs toward the particle surfaces and upon carbonization the final CNDs with abundant oxygen-containing groups on the surface are obtained. Besides, compared with the solid products, the aromatization and carbonization reaction may be incomplete and some defects may be present on the surface, which can contribute to excellent PL properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Texture and surface properties of CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: TEM images and histograms of the size distribution of the CNDs are presented in Fig. 2 . This shows that the CNDs are spherical and well dispersed with a lattice structure. A count of 100 CNDs gave a mean diameter of 30.2nm.

XPS was conducted for an analysis of the chemical composition of the CNDs. We found C (71.7%) and O (28.3%) in the CNDs, as shown in Fig. 3 (a). The high C content confirms that a condensed material was produced. The C1s spectra were fit to four curves and attributed to aliphatic and aromatic groups (C–H, C–C, CC) (285.3eV), hydroxyl groups (C–OR) (285.7), carbonyl groups (CO) (286.7), and carboxylic acids or lactones (–COOR) (289.4), respectively [14,19]. These results indicate the presence of an aromatic structure and abundant oxygen-containing groups on the surface.

FTIR analysis indicated the presence of additional functional groups on the surface of the CNDs. A carbonyl group FTIR analysis indicated the presence of additional functional groups on the surface of the CNDs. A carbonyl group (CO) band at 1705cm−1, a C–O peak at 1000–1400cm−1, and an –OH band at 3370–3237cm−1 appeared indicating the presence of hydrophilic groups. This explains the good water-solubility of the CNDs [12]. Condensed CC groups were present because of the band at 1667cm−1 with aromatic C–H out of plane bending vibrations at 875–750cm−1 indicating the presence of aromatic rings. These results agree with the XPS analysis. The reductive OH or CHO groups that are covalently bound to the carbon framework will improve the hydrophilicity and the stability of the CNDs [20].

A broad reflection peak centred at 21.5° is present in the XRD spectrum, indicating the amorphous structure of the CNDs. Using Braggs law, the interlayer spacing of the (002) diffraction peak was found to be 0.413nm, which is larger than that of graphite (0.34nm), and this can be attributed to the oxygen-containing groups [21].

UV–vis and PL spectra were used to investigate the optical properties of the CNDs. The aqueous solution was light yellow with good dispersibility. A UV–vis absorption spectrum gave a weak and broad peak around 250–300nm, as shown in Fig. 4 a. This is attributed to the complex electron transition on the surface, the π conjugated aromatic system and the n–π* transition of the carbonyl and other oxygen-containing groups [22,23]. This is consistent with the FTIR analysis. The aqueous solution exhibits bright green luminescence upon 365nm UV irradiation and this is observable with naked eyes, as shown in Fig. 3d. Emission spectra around 400–600nm with a peak at 452nm are observed upon excitation at 360nm. A peak centered at 364nm in the excitation spectrum is present with an emission wavelength of 450nm. PL spectra also show that the maximum emission and intensity have a direct relationship with the excitation wavelength, and this is a PL characteristic of C dots [24]. Upon changing the excitation wavelength from 360 to 500nm, the emission peak red-shifts from 451 to 587nm with a gradual decrease in intensity. Therefore, visible emission from the CNPs over the blue-to-red wavelength range can be obtained using the same sample under UV and visible excitation. We thus used 360nm as the excitation wavelength for subsequent experiments. The PL properties can be attributed to surface energy traps formed via incomplete reaction, and the radiative recombination of electrons and holes [25]. Additionally, polycyclic aromatic compounds within the CNDs may be responsible for the excellent PL properties [26]. The quantum yield was calculated to be about 24% using Rhodamine B in ethanol as the standard, and the yield was found to be higher than that of most reported CNPs [27,28].

Fig. 5 shows optical fluorescence microscope images of CNPs. The samples were prepared by placing a drop of solution on a glass slip and then covering with a cover glass. The CNPs exhibited different emission colours including blue, green and red when excited at 360nm, 460nm and 550nm, respectively. Many carbon nanomaterials exhibit this phenomenon, and this may derive from the different properties of surface defects under various excitation wavelengths [29].

The relationship between the emission and the pH of the CNDs was studied using a sodium bicarbonate and sodium carbonate buffer solution. The colour ranged from light yellow to deep yellow as the pH was varied from 4.0 to 10.0 as shown in Fig. 6 . This may be caused by a surface property change and CNDs electronic transitions. With an increase in pH the intensity of the emission peak decreases without a change in wavelength, as reported before [13]. Oxygen-containing groups on the surface impart acid properties while at high pH values the solution environment changes. The negative charge density increases on the surface and influences the π-th*, n–e* electronic transition, as well as the chemical properties and surface defects on the CNDs, which leads to fluorescence quenching. A linear calibration plot was obtained with a high correlation coefficient of R 2 =0.98, which indicates a potential application in the measurement of wastewater pH.

The CNDs exhibit good photostability toward UV irradiation and in a strongly ionic solution (NaCl), as shown in Table 1 . The intensity decreased about 2.6% from 381.94 to 372.00 after 5h of irradiation with 365nm UV light. The intensity remained almost constant (<3% change) in a NaCl solution, even at a concentration of 500mmoll−1. Their high stability and water-solubility make them suitable for applications in biological labelling and imaging.

As CNDs can be excited by UV and part of visible-light (300–550nm), they can potentially be used as photocatalyst modifiers. TiO2 is a widely used photocatalyst for the removal of environmental pollution as it is a good oxidant, is highly photocatalytic, chemically stable and has low toxicity [30,31]. The major obstacle to its effective use is its narrow spectral response range as only UV irradiation is effective, and it comprises only 5% of incident sunlight. The CNDs investigated here are rich in oxygen-containing groups, and are easily attached in a stable manner to the surface of TiO2 [32]. CNDs were thus combined with TiO2 in a composite system, it can be seen from Fig. 7 (a), due to the low magnification of SEM, no CNDs can be observed. However, HRTEM confirmed that CNDs with the size of dozens of nanometers have attached on the surface of TiO2 successfully. Its photocatalytic activity was evaluated by its ability to decompose MB under visible light irradiation, as shown in Fig. 7(b). Compared with pure CNDs and TiO2 as catalysts for MB degradation, we only found less than 17% target reduction, and this was mainly because of adsorption. The CNDs/TiO2 composite gave efficient catalysis and a near 100% MB degradation, indicating an interaction between the CNDs and TiO2. The proposed mechanism is as follows (Fig. 7c): the CNDs with abundant oxygen-containing groups are sensitizer, when irradiation by visible light they can act as a photosensitizer and absorb photons. Photons with adequate energy can excite electrons in the HOMO, thus transferring them to the LUMO of the CNDs. Free electrons as well energy can then be transferred to the conduction band of TiO2 [33]. These electrons react with oxidants (O2) adsorbed on TiO2 to produce active oxygen radicals (O2−), which can further degrade MB into small molecules.

To determine the stability of prepared catalysts, CNDs/TiO2 composite was used for several photocatalytic runs. The results in Fig. 7(d) show that the activity of CNDs/TiO2 only slightly decreased and 82% MB degradation was still achieved after three runs, thus, CNDs/TiO2 retained high activity and confirm the high stability of the system.

We separated water-soluble carbon nano dots from the liquid by-products of the one-pot hydrothermal carbonization of pentosan, which is a by-product of pulp refining. The as-prepared CNDs had a sphere structure of 30nm in size with abundant oxygen-containing groups on their surface. They were characterized by excellent photoluminescence (PL), excitation-dependence and pH sensitivity. Additionally, they are highly stable toward UV–vis irradiation and in ionic solutions. The CNDs/TiO2 system was found to be an effective photocatalyst, and the CNDs on the surface of TiO2 act as a photosensitizer. They have the wide spectral response range of TiO2 in the UV region, in addition to spectral response in the visible-light region (400–550nm). 98.1% MB degradation under visible irradiation was achieved.

Water-soluble carbon nano dots (CNDs) with high fluorescence are synthesized from liquid by-products of hydrothermal carbonization of pentosan, the by-products of pulp refining. CNDs can be effectively used as photo sensitizer in the CNDs/TiO2 system for methyl blue (MB) degradation under visible light irradiation, spectral response range of CNDs/TiO2 system can be widen from only UV region to part visible-light region (400–550nm).

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Research Fund for the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2572014EB01), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31170545) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2572014AB17).
